referral_details.referral:
49-year-old female with history of left-sided invasive ductal carcinoma, ER+/PR+, HER2-, diagnosed March 2024. Underwent left modified radical mastectomy with axillary node dissection (14 nodes removed, 3 positive) on 2024-05-02 at Toronto General Hospital. Completed adjuvant chemotherapy (AC-T regimen) from 2024-06-10 to 2024-10-01. Currently on adjuvant endocrine therapy (letrozole 2.5 mg daily since 2024-11-01).

Primary reasons for referral:
1. Post-mastectomy shoulder dysfunction and pain: Progressive tightness, pulling sensation, and reduced active range of motion in the left shoulder since surgery, worsened after radiation. Difficulty with overhead activities (e.g., reaching for dishes, blow-drying hair, fastening bra behind back). Reports pain 5–7/10 at end range, described as a deep ache with intermittent sharp pulling along the lateral chest wall and axilla.

2. Early lymphedema / arm heaviness: Intermittent swelling and heaviness in the left upper arm and forearm, especially toward the end of the day or after prolonged computer work or carrying grocery bags. Mild pitting noted on exam at forearm; no open wounds or skin breakdown. Patient is very anxious about progression of lymphedema due to family history (mother had chronic arm swelling after breast cancer surgery).

3. Post-radiation soft tissue tightness and scar adhesions: Completed left chest wall and regional nodal radiation (25 fractions + 5 boost) from 2024-10-21 to 2024-12-02. Now with significant tightness along the anterior chest wall and axilla, cording-like sensation when abducting the arm. Well-healed transverse mastectomy scar but with palpable adhesions and restricted skin glide, particularly medially and toward the axilla.

4. Postural changes and functional limitations: Notable protective posture with rounded shoulders and slight forward head position, guarding on the left side. Patient reports difficulty sleeping on the left, discomfort wearing a bra or prosthesis for more than a few hours, and fatigue in the upper back and neck by late afternoon. She works as a senior project manager in IT, primarily desk/computer-based, and cannot comfortably maintain proper ergonomic posture due to tightness and pain.

Relevant imaging and medical history:
- No evidence of metastatic disease on staging scans (CT chest/abdomen and bone scan April 2024).
- Non-smoker, drinks alcohol socially (1–2 glasses of wine on weekends).
- Past medical history: mild asthma (well controlled, uses salbutamol inhaler PRN), generalized anxiety disorder (on sertraline 50 mg daily), history of low back pain related to desk work.
- Medications: letrozole 2.5 mg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily, vitamin D3 2000 IU daily, occasional acetaminophen for pain.

Goals of referral:
- Comprehensive post-mastectomy and post-radiation rehabilitation program to restore left shoulder and upper extremity range of motion and strength.
- Assessment and management of early lymphedema risk, including education on self-management, compression options, and manual lymph drainage as appropriate.
- Scar tissue and soft-tissue mobilization to improve chest wall mobility and reduce pain and pulling sensations.
- Postural retraining and ergonomic guidance tailored to her computer-based work, including home exercise program.
- Education regarding safe progression of physical activity; patient is motivated to return to yoga (modified) and light strength training but is unsure what is safe.

Patient is highly engaged and motivated, reports good social support (lives with spouse and 16-year-old daughter in a two-story home in Toronto). No current signs of infection, no open wounds, no contraindications to gentle manual therapy or exercise identified. Please assess and treat as indicated within breast cancer rehabilitation scope of practice.
referral_details.comments:
Patient reports that the tightness and pulling along the left chest and underarm area are the most distressing symptoms day to day, followed closely by the fear of her arm swelling getting worse. She notes that she tends to overuse her right arm for almost everything now, which is starting to cause right-sided neck and shoulder fatigue and occasional tension headaches.

Functionally, she can manage basic self-care independently but avoids tasks like changing the duvet cover, lifting heavier groceries, or carrying laundry baskets up the stairs because these activities flare her pain and sense of heaviness in the left arm. She has stopped doing yoga entirely since surgery and feels this has significantly affected her mood and sense of normalcy. She is very open to learning detailed home exercises, self-massage techniques, and any strategies that will help her feel more in control of her recovery.

No current open areas or drainage at the surgical site; scar is fully epithelialized and pale. Skin in the radiation field is mildly hyperpigmented and remains somewhat sensitive to pressure and friction (e.g., bra straps, seatbelt). She uses fragrance-free moisturizer daily and is careful about sun exposure.

She has private insurance coverage through her employer that may partially cover physiotherapy and massage therapy visits; she is willing to attend regular in-person sessions and follow a structured home program. Prefers clear written instructions and diagrams she can keep in a binder. She has her own foam roller, light resistance bands, and a yoga mat at home and is comfortable using them with appropriate guidance.

No history of blood clots, no pacemaker, and no current anticoagulation therapy. She does experience mild hot flashes and joint stiffness in the mornings related to endocrine therapy, so morning sessions might require a longer warm-up period.

Please feel free to contact referring physician if any concerns arise during rehabilitation or if you suspect progression of lymphedema requiring further medical evaluation.
